narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The system we use looks at patterns from thousands of past students—things like their grades, test scores, family backgrounds, and study plans—to predict if they'd pass the bar exam on their first try. For you, it estimated a 48% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score of 22, well below the typical 37 seen in the data, making the model worry about your readiness for the rigors ahead. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, as students from similar backgrounds often outperform expectations through grit and support networks. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, slightly under the average 3.2, also nudged things toward a pass prediction compared to others. Your Year 1 school decile of 5 was right around average and helped balance the scales positively too. However, your Year 3 decile of 3, lower than the usual 5.5, raised some flags about later academic momentum. Choosing part-time status, uncommon for just 7% of students who mostly go full-time, added a small push toward the failure prediction, likely due to concerns over divided focus. Overall, your profile stands out as resilient in areas like family background but trails typical admits in key test and grade metrics that the model weighs heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many in similar situations, including women and Black students like you, have succeeded by targeting improvements. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades, and exploring full-time options if feasible; then reapply with a stronger package to show your growth. You've got this—keep pushing forward.
